title: OpenAI backtracks on new Pentagon contract, says it will revise deal
fullText:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman says he regrets rushing through a AI supply deal with the Defense Department, announcing plans to amend the contract to preclude use of its tools by the intelligence agencies for domestic mass surveillance purposes. File Photo by Anna Rose Layden/UPI | License Photo

March 3 (UPI) -- OpenAI said it will revise its agreement to supply the U.S. Department of Defense with AI to include provisions banning the use of its tools by the intelligence agencies for domestic mass surveillance purposes.
The existing deal announced on Friday had been hurried and reflected badly on the firm after OpenAI stepped in to replace Anthropic within hours of it being dropped for refusing to allow its technology to be used for surveillance and autonomous weapons, CEO Sam Altman told staff on Monday.

"We shouldn't have rushed to get this out on Friday. The issues are super complex, and demand clear communication. We were genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome, but I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy," Altman said.
According to an internal document reposted by Altman, changes to the contract included new wording explicitly stating that "the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals."

"For the avoidance of doubt, the Department understands the limitation to prohibit deliberate tracking, surveillance, or monitoring of U.S. persons or nationals, including through the procurement or use of commercially acquired personal or identifiable information," it added.
Altman insisted the Pentagon had agreed agencies such as the National Security Agency would not use OpenAI's tools because there were many areas the technology wasn't ready for and that there was insufficient understanding of the "tradeoffs required for safety."
He said technical safeguards was an area that OpenAI and the Pentagon would work on jointly.
Monday's move came after a backlash both from the public, online, and from OpenAI and Google staff.
Industry data showed a sharp rise in users uninstalling OpenAI's ChatGPT app and a significant, albeit smaller, jump in downloads of Anthropic's Claude app on Friday and Saturday, rocketing Claude into the No. 1 spot on the U.S. App Store, where it remained as of Monday.
An open letter signed by more than 900 employees of OpenAI and Google called on the two companies to join forces to resist the Department of Defense's "demands for permission to use our models for domestic mass surveillance and autonomously killing people without human oversight."

"They're trying to divide each company with fear that the other will give in. That strategy only works if none of us know where the others stand. This letter serves to create shared understanding and solidarity in the face of this pressure from the Department of War," they wrote.
In its original announcement of the deal on Friday, OpenAI said its work with the DoD was defined by red lines that included no use of its technology for mass domestic surveillance, direction of autonomous weapons systems or for use in high-stakes automated decisions.
It also stated that it had asked the department to make the same deal available to all AI companies.
However, critics questioned how OpenAI managed to so easily negotiate an ethically acceptable contract, where Anthropic, in which Google holds a 14% stake, failed.
"OpenAI employees' default assumption here should unfortunately be that OpenAI caved + framed it as not caving, and screwed Anthropic while framing it as helping them," said OpenAI's former policy research head Miles Brundage.
"To be clear, OAI is a complex org, and I think many people involved in this worked hard for what they consider a fair outcome. Some others I do not trust at all, particularly as it relates to dealings with government and politics," he added.

Anthropic was the first AI developer whose models were put to work across the Pentagon's classified operations after a deal in 2025 but the partnership soured after the company asked for assurances its technology wouldn't be used against U.S. citizens or for autonomous weapons.
Following a dressing down on Friday over its refusal to cooperate, in which Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said he was designating Anthropic a supply-chain threat, President Donald Trump announced a ban on federal agencies using Anthropic's artificial intelligence solutions with immediate effect.
He said there would be a six-month phase-out period for the Pentagon contract to give it time to transition to alternative systems and warned Anthropic that it had better be helpful, or face the "Full Power of the Presidency to make them comply, with major civil and criminal consequences to follow."
"WE will decide the fate of our Country -- NOT some out-of-control, Radical Left AI company run by people who have no idea what the real World is all about," he wrote in a post lambasting the company on his Truth Social platform.

 
 
 
 
 
 
 President Donald Trump and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz hold a bilateral meeting in the Oval Office at the White House on Tuesday. Trump was meeting with Merz just days after the United States and Israel launched widespread attacks on Iran, killing Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and other senior leaders in airstrikes across the region. Photo by Samuel Corum/UPI | License Photo Read More
OpenAI reaches deal with Pentagon after Trump drops Anthropic
Trump bans U.S. government use of Anthropic AI products
Chinese hackers hijack Anthropic AI in 1st 'large scale' cyberattack
summary: Tech firm OpenAI regrets rushing a deal with the Pentagon and will amend it to ban use of its AI for surveillance and killing without human oversight.
